title: Arthur
summary: The repository XML is a local representation of University of Michigan CME item CME00055, keyed from Frederick J. Furnivall's 1869 second edition. That edition revises his 1864 transcription of the poem's sole witness, the Red Book of Bath, formerly Longleat House MS 55 and now British Library Add MS 89789. The complete 157-canvas manuscript surrogate is cached, with Arthur mapped inside it to fols. 42v-46r, canvases 95-102. Later critical editions and source studies are collateral scholarship, not hidden ancestors of the CME text.

evidence.summary: Describes the repository's working source snapshot as drawn from the Corpus of Middle English.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source XML, CME00055.xml.
evidence.locator: IDG, FILEDESC, TEMPHEAD, title-page DIV1, poem DIV1, and commentary DIV1
evidence.summary: Establishes the repository artifact, CME identifiers, Furnivall title and second-edition statement, complete poem text, printed-page and manuscript-location milestones, editorial notation, and post-poem commentary.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse.
evidence.summary: The collection describes 289 manually keyed and in-house-proofread texts from public-domain editions and applies a No Copyright-United States statement. The CME00055 stable item route was Cloudflare-challenged during automated review.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., Arthur, 2nd ed., EETS OS 2 (1869), complete component in Internet Archive bound-volume scan.
evidence.locator: physical PDF pages 8-34; poem at physical PDF pages 14-32, printed pages 1-19
evidence.summary: Direct inspection confirms the explicit Second Edition, 1869 title statement with retained 1864 imprint; revised preface; direct manuscript-copy claim; numbering through line 642; handling of contractions, side-notes, and punctuation; WORDS list; and the larger composite binding.
Identifies the Harvard source, Google digitization, Internet Archive identifier and ARK, derivative files, and possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library catalog record 2003, Arthur, EETS OS 2.
evidence.summary: Catalogs the item as the second edition of 1869 with London 1864 publication data, vii, 19, [1] pages, and later 1889 and 1895 reprints.

evidence.citation: Early English Text Society, List of All Publications, 2026.
evidence.summary: The official current list gives Arthur, editor F. J. Furnivall, publication year 1864, ISBN 9780859918008, and prices GBP 15 and USD 24.95.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 89789, The Red Book of Bath.
evidence.locator: scope and content; date range; access; physical characteristics; custodial history
evidence.summary: Confirms the current shelfmark and LARK, former Longleat House MS 55 designation, unique Arthur at fols. 42v-46r inside the Latin Brut, 1412-1430 Bath production, parchment extent and medieval binding, Somerset dialect association, April 2025 acquisition, public digital content, and restricted physical-access route.

evidence.citation: British Library IIIF Presentation 3 manifest for Add MS 89789.
evidence.summary: Provides exact canvas/image-service URLs, labels for the complete physical object, British Library provider attribution, RAQ ID, and the usage statement Public Domain in most countries other than the UK.
notes: The manifest's rights URL returned 404 during review, but the usage statement and RAQ identifier are embedded in the preserved manifest.

evidence.citation: Local integrity and visual audit of the complete Add MS 89789 IIIF bundle.
evidence.summary: ZIP integrity and all 157 images passed. The sequence includes both covers, endleaves, fols. 1r-69v, interior boards, back cover, spine, fore-edge, head-edge, and tail-edge. Visual checks confirm Arthur's two-column Middle English verse begins on fol. 42v, continues through fol. 46r, and the decorated Latin Brut resumes on fol. 46v.

evidence.citation: Project Gutenberg ebook 16845, Arthur, by Frederick J. Furnivall.
evidence.summary: Provides a complete first-edition-derived digital text, production/update metadata, the public-domain-in-USA statement, and Gutenberg redistribution terms. Its title and unrevised preface distinguish it from the 1869 source branch.

evidence.citation: Mildred Willingham Bryan, A Critical Edition and Verse Translation of Arthur (PhD diss., University of Alabama, 1977).
evidence.locator: introduction pp. 1-33; critical text pp. 38-66; notes pp. 67-75; translation pp. 76-105
evidence.summary: Confirms the poem's untitled manuscript state, anonymous authorship, historical dating and dialect scholarship, Furnivall edition history, Bryan's own critical-text numbering through 644, meter and rhyme, complete translation, and institutional all-rights-reserved statement. Bryan's numbering differs from Furnivall/CME because of the separately treated Pater/Ave prayer material.

evidence.citation: Marije Pots and Erik Kooper, 'Arthur: A New Critical Edition of the Fifteenth-Century Middle English Verse Chronicle,' The Medieval Chronicle 7 (2011): 239-266.
evidence.locator: public preview pp. 239-240; full article pp. 239-266
evidence.summary: Supports the modern critical-edition identity, verse-chronicle classification, sole-witness context, and probable 1350-1400 composition range. Only the two-page public preview is cached.

evidence.citation: Erik Kooper and Julia Marvin, 'A Source for the Middle English Poem Arthur,' Arthuriana 22.4 (Winter 2012; institutional record 2013): 25-45.
evidence.summary: The source study identifies the Oldest Version of the Anglo-Norman Prose Brut as Arthur's most important source. The institutional record supplies authorship, journal, volume, issue, and pages but records publication as 2013.

evidence.citation: Erik Kooper, 'Longleat House MS 55: An Unacknowledged Brut Manuscript?' in The Prose Brut and Other Late Medieval Chronicles (York Medieval Press, 2016), 65-84.
evidence.locator: pp. 65-84, especially fols. 42v-46r and the Arthur/Latin-Brut boundary
evidence.summary: Analyzes the complete codex and its Latin Brut, locates Arthur precisely, reviews dialect and editorial history, and restates the Kooper-Marvin finding that the Oldest Version of the Anglo-Norman Prose Brut was the poem's most important source.

rights.component: The medieval Arthur text and other medieval contents as intellectual works

editorial_practices.representation: Expanded letters printed in italics; generally encoded by HI elements with REND=italic in CME00055
editorial_practices.description: Furnivall states that expansions of contractions are printed in italics. These italics usually mark editorially supplied letters, not manuscript emphasis or ellipsis.
notes: The complete manuscript facsimile now permits each expansion to be checked against the exact abbreviation form on fols. 42v-46r.

editorial_practices.representation: Manuscript stops printed in parentheses within a broader editorial punctuation system
editorial_practices.description: Furnivall explicitly identifies parenthesized stops as manuscript punctuation, enabling readers to distinguish them from his ordinary editorial punctuation.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Latin and vernacular manuscript marginal annotations, many rubricated or framed
editorial_practices.representation: Side-notes set in italic or Clarendon type and encoded primarily as NOTE PLACE=marg
editorial_practices.description: Furnivall preserves manuscript side-notes but translates their visual placement and color into nineteenth-century typographic distinctions; the XML preserves semantic note placement, not the complete manuscript page layout.
notes: Facsimile canvases 95-102 show the two-column layout, red line fillers, boxed/rubricated annotations, and marginal positions that print typography cannot reproduce exactly.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Furnivall's complete second-edition component within the scanned bound volume
editorial_practices.representation: Title page, poem on printed pages 1-19, and brief following commentary are encoded; the revised preface and final WORDS list are omitted
editorial_practices.description: The digital item represents the poem and selected edition framing rather than every page of the physical EETS publication or composite bound scan.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Printed lineation, page breaks, manuscript folio/column references, marginal notes, and editorial italics
editorial_practices.representation: L, PB, MILESTONE, NOTE, HI, Q, and CLOSER elements preserve textual structure and selected edition signals
editorial_practices.description: The encoding is manually keyed and proofread from the public-domain edition. It retains many editorial signals but should not be treated as a diplomatic transcription of manuscript layout.
notes: Leaf MILESTONE elements also occur inside post-poem editorial notes; direct poem boundaries should be taken from the manuscript mapping, not from an unfiltered search for leaf milestones.

open_questions.question: What exact reuse terms apply in the United Kingdom to the British Library Add MS 89789 IIIF images?
next_steps: Ask British Library Reference or Rights Services to interpret RAQ ID 16.2/20170119 for republication in a public Git repository and print-on-demand research context.
Request a durable replacement for the manifest's currently broken image-reuse link.
Keep the whole-manuscript ZIP gitignored until the provider's UK carve-out is resolved.
notes: The manifest says Public Domain in most countries other than the UK and therefore does not support an unqualified worldwide-publication claim.

open_questions.question: What exact textual readings changed between Furnivall's 1864 first edition and his 1869 second edition?
next_steps: Collate Project Gutenberg's first-edition text against the scanned 1869 second edition and the manuscript facsimile.
Separate revised introductory claims from changes to the Middle English transcription itself.

open_questions.question: Can the precise textual branch or lost exemplar behind Arthur's Oldest-Version Anglo-Norman Prose Brut source be identified?
next_steps: Consult the complete Kooper-Marvin article and its diagnostic comparisons.
Distinguish a modern comparison witness such as Bodleian MS Wood empt. 8 from the unidentified historical exemplar used by the poet.

open_questions.question: How should the British Library catalog's references to fols. 70r-v be reconciled with its stated extent of 69 folios and IIIF labels ending at fol. 69v?
next_steps: Ask the British Library whether the maps are unnumbered, historically foliated 70, or cataloged by a superseded foliation.
Preserve current IIIF canvas labels unless the institution issues a correction.
notes: The discrepancy does not affect Arthur's securely labeled range at fols. 42v-46r.

The complete manuscript considered in the study

subject.description: The OS 2 entry in the complete publication list

review_notes: Every access and rights statement is scoped to its named entity or component; medieval text, nineteenth-century edition, modern encoding, provider scan, and repository output are separate layers.

The complete publicly obtainable physical manuscript object is cached: covers, boards, endleaves, every manuscript leaf, spine, fore-edge, head-edge, and tail-edge. The work locator does not crop that bundle.
The British Library IIIF manifest's own labels establish Arthur at canvases 95-102 (fols. 42v-46r), with the surrounding Latin Brut retained at canvases 94 and 103 and throughout the complete bundle.
The Furnivall PDF is a complete scan of a composite bound volume; Arthur's edition component is mapped separately rather than treating adjacent EETS works as part of CME00055.
Current public access and rights findings are date-bounded to 2026-07-12.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00055
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID all read CME00055.

The XML's 642 L elements correspond to Furnivall's numbering through line 642; one separate Q element represents the unnumbered printed prayer line Pater noster / Aue /. Bryan's later edition numbers through 644 after treating that material differently.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00055
entities.description: A keyed, proofread, and structurally marked-up digital representation of Furnivall's second edition.
notes: The stable item route was protected by a Cloudflare challenge during review; the collection route and local pinned XML remained available.

notes: Project Gutenberg ebook 16845 represents this first-edition state: its title and preface lack the explicit second-edition statement and Furnivall's March 1869 revision note.

entities.label: Furnivall 1869 second edition of Arthur

date_statements.label: second-edition publication
date_statements.notes: The title page says Second Edition, 1869 while retaining the roman-numeral 1864 imprint.

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., Arthur: A Short Sketch of His Life and History in English Verse of the First Half of the Fifteenth Century, 2nd ed., EETS OS 2 (London: Trübner, 1869; title-page imprint 1864).
bibliographic.title: Arthur: A Short Sketch of His Life and History in English Verse of the First Half of the Fifteenth Century
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 2

notes: This is the immediate printed ancestor named and represented by CME00055.
Furnivall's preface is signed August 30, 1864 and adds Revised, March 2, 1869. Later reprints appeared in 1889 and 1895.

entities.label: British Library Add MS 89789, The Red Book of Bath
entities.description: The unique manuscript witness of Arthur. The poem interrupts a Latin Brut at fols. 42v-46r in a composite civic compilation made for officials of Bath.

holding.notes: Purchased by the British Library from the Trustees of the Longleat House and Chattels Settlement in April 2025.

physical_description.extent: iii modern paper endleaves + 69 manuscript folios; 1 volume
physical_description.format: Gothic cursive minuscule; Arthur in two columns
notes: Contemporary oak-board binding on five bands with a reddish-pink leather chemise and metal bosses.
A hinged cavity in the upper board formerly held scales and weights for measuring gold.
Arthur occupies fols. 42v-46r; the Latin Brut runs fols. 35v-42r and resumes at 46v-53v.
The British Library catalogs two maps at fols. 70r-v, while its physical description says 69 folios and the IIIF sequence labels through fol. 69v; this minor foliation discrepancy remains unresolved.
Bath production and Somerset witness dialect are not treated as proof that the anonymous poem was composed in Bath.

entities.label: British Library complete digital facsimile of Add MS 89789
entities.description: A 157-canvas color IIIF surrogate of the complete physical manuscript object, from front cover through all endleaves and manuscript leaves to the back cover, spine, fore-edge, head-edge, and tail-edge.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 3 manifest and JPEG color images
notes: Arthur is a portion of this complete surrogate, at fols. 42v-46r, canvases 95-102; the cached object is deliberately not cropped to those eight canvases.

The downloader requested width 1800. The provider returned its full-profile derivative for canvases 1-155, generally about 1390 by 2000 pixels, while the head- and tail-edge images honored approximately 1800-pixel width.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan containing Furnivall's 1869 Arthur
entities.description: A complete 267-page PDF scan of a Harvard/Google composite bound volume containing Furnivall's second edition of Arthur and adjacent EETS publications.
notes: Arthur's complete edition component occupies physical PDF pages 8-34; its poem occupies physical PDF pages 14-32, corresponding to printed pages 1-19.
The complete bound scan was cached, preserving the adjacent publications rather than extracting only Arthur.

entities.label: Project Gutenberg ebook 16845, Arthur
entities.description: A public HTML/EPUB/plain-text encoding of Furnivall's first-edition state, produced by Distributed Proofreaders and Project Gutenberg.
notes: Released 2005-10-10 and last updated 2020-12-12 according to the ebook record.
Its omission of the 1869 second-edition statement and revised preface distinguishes this branch from the U-M encoding.

entities.label: Bryan 1977 critical edition and verse translation

date_statements.label: dissertation date
date_statements.notes: Some later bibliographies cite 1978; the dissertation title page and institutional record give 1977.

bibliographic.citation: Mildred Willingham Bryan, A Critical Edition and Verse Translation of Arthur (PhD diss., University of Alabama, 1977).
bibliographic.title: A Critical Edition and Verse Translation of Arthur

notes: Includes introduction, critical text, explanatory notes, verse translation, glossary, index of proper names, and bibliography.

Bryan's critical text numbers the last line 644, whereas Furnivall/CME end at 642; the divergence begins where Bryan separately displays and counts Pater Noster and Ave material that Furnivall prints as one unnumbered prayer line.
This later edition was not a source for the nineteenth-century Furnivall edition or the U-M encoding.

bibliographic.title: Arthur: A New Critical Edition of the Fifteenth-Century Middle English Verse Chronicle

notes: Only the two-page publisher preview was publicly downloadable without subscription during this review.
